title: Refactoring
use_for: simplifying existing code without changing behavior
removing overengineered layers and unused abstractions
cleaning residual code after feature removal
auditing hidden stale couplings before deletion
reducing maintenance cost while preserving contracts
do_not_use_for: bug fixes where root cause is not understood
new feature design as the primary goal
performance redesign unless performance is explicitly the owner
large rewrites with no validation path
rules: define the behavior contract before changing structure
separate mechanical cleanup from semantic changes
remove abstractions only after call sites and ownership are understood
treat stale feature remnants as regression risks until reachability is checked
keep refactors small enough to validate and review
procedure: State the refactor goal and what behavior must remain unchanged.
Map owners, call sites, lifecycle hooks, dataflow, configuration, tests, and integration boundaries.
Classify code as active, stale, transitional, duplicated, over-abstracted, or unknown.
Choose a narrow transformation: inline, delete, split, move, rename, consolidate, or isolate.
Apply the transformation in a reviewable sequence.
Run validation focused on unchanged behavior and removed coupling.
Leave notes for any suspicious but unresolved code that should not be deleted yet.

primary_when: the request is primarily about simplifying, cleaning, restructuring, deleting stale code, or reducing overengineering in existing code
the task is to remove obsolete feature remnants or hidden couplings safely
must_not_own_when: there is an active bug whose root cause is still unknown and investigation must happen first
the task is primarily a new feature, UI design, performance architecture, or reverse-engineering task

solves: how to simplify existing systems without introducing regressions
how to remove dead or stale code safely
how to detect hidden links left after feature deletion
how to reduce overengineering while preserving useful boundaries
does_not_solve: unknown bug root-cause discovery by itself
domain redesign where behavior is intentionally changing

core_principles: Refactoring changes structure while preserving behavior.
Deletion is a behavior change unless reachability and side effects are understood.
Overengineering is not abstraction count; it is abstraction cost without current runtime or maintenance value.
Residual code is dangerous because it can remain connected through lifecycle hooks, configuration, serialization, reflection, events, tests, editor scripts, or generated data.
The safest refactor sequence reduces uncertainty before it reduces code.
constraints: Do not mix broad cleanup with unrelated feature changes.
Do not remove adapters, configs, serialized fields, events, or lifecycle hooks based only on text search.
Do not collapse boundaries that still protect ownership, lifecycle, validation, or integration contracts.
Do not rename public or serialized surfaces without migration or compatibility review.
Do not continue refactoring if validation exposes unexplained behavior changes.
decision_rules: If the current behavior is unclear, characterize it before refactoring.
If code looks unused, check call sites, configuration, serialization, reflection, editor scripts, tests, generated files, and external entry points.
If an abstraction has one caller and no ownership or validation value, consider inlining or merging it.
If an abstraction protects lifecycle, domain boundary, diagnostics, or testability, keep it unless its cost is proven harmful.
If feature deletion left remnants, remove from the outermost entry points inward: UI/config/registration, lifecycle hooks, data paths, tests, docs, then core code.
If a refactor reveals a bug, switch to bug-investigation for that failure before continuing cleanup.

preferred_patterns: call-site and lifecycle reachability maps before deletion
public surface and serialized data compatibility review
separate commits or patches for mechanical moves and semantic changes
explicit removal checklist for deleted features
avoid_patterns: deleting code solely because it has no direct text references
collapsing boundaries that still encode ownership or lifecycle rules
mixing renames, moves, behavior changes, and bug fixes in one opaque patch
leaving obsolete config, docs, tests, or registration paths after deleting implementation

recipes.name: Reduce overengineering
recipes.when: Use when a subsystem has too many layers, abstractions, or indirections for its current responsibilities.
steps: Identify the real current responsibilities and active callers.
Mark which abstractions protect a useful boundary and which only forward calls.
Inline or merge pass-through layers one at a time.
Keep boundary names where they encode ownership, lifecycle, or validation.
Validate behavior after each meaningful simplification.

recipes.name: Remove feature remnants
recipes.when: Use after a feature was deleted or disabled but related code may still be connected.
steps: List all entry points: registration, config, UI, commands, lifecycle hooks, tests, docs, assets, serialization, and generated data.
Remove or gate entry points before deleting deep implementation.
Search for stale identifiers and indirect references.
Run compile/tests/smoke path and inspect logs for missing references.
Remove tests and docs only when they describe the deleted behavior rather than preserved contracts.

recipes.name: Audit hidden coupling
recipes.when: Use when old code may still affect behavior through indirect links.
steps: Search direct references and also registration tables, event subscriptions, config keys, serialized fields, reflection strings, editor scripts, and generated assets.
Trace lifecycle: initialization, update, fixed update, shutdown, suspend, resume, disposal.
Trace dataflow: who writes, who reads, who caches, who publishes.
Disable or remove the suspected link in the shallowest safe location first.
Validate that expected behavior remains and the obsolete path is no longer active.

pitfalls: Deleting stale-looking code that is still referenced by serialized assets or registration.
Calling a rewrite a refactor while behavior is intentionally changing.
Removing an abstraction that was the only place enforcing lifecycle or ownership.
Leaving old tests or configs that re-enable removed paths later.
Refactoring through a failing test without first understanding whether the test reveals a real bug.
review_checklist: Were call sites and lifecycle hooks mapped before deletion?
Are mechanical changes separated from semantic changes?
Did validation prove behavior stayed stable or intentionally changed?
Were unresolved suspicious links documented instead of silently ignored?

references.title: Refactoring
references.interpretation: Use this first for behavior-preserving cleanup, overengineering reduction, and safe deletion.
references.why: It encodes the practical refactoring discipline for existing project code.

references.title: Stale Coupling Audit
references.interpretation: Use when old feature remnants or hidden obsolete links can cause regressions.
references.why: Hidden coupling is one of the most painful failure modes in large evolving projects.
